title: T-017 stale comment contradicts cycle-6 H002 — but assertion still holds
description: T-017 covers the 'enabledPlugins key absent' branch and was authored in cycle 1 with the comment 'Silent disable path — must not throw, warning is optional but typically absent.' Under cycle-6 H002 the implementation now ALWAYS emits an output-style-plugin-not-enabled warning on this branch (see outputStyle.ts L51-58), so the comment is factually wrong. The assertion itself only checks result.ok===false, which still passes — but a future maintainer who reads the comment would believe the silent-disable contract still holds and might reintroduce a bare {ok:false} return.
impact: Documentation drift. Risk that a future change accidentally removes the H002 warning from the absent-key branch because the test comment claims silence is acceptable. The orchestrator amendment list in synthesis-notes covers T-014 (the false-key branch) but not T-017 (the absent-key branch); both should have been rebased symmetrically.
recommendation: Update the comment to read '// Cycle 6 H002: this branch now emits output-style-plugin-not-enabled; T-306/T-307 in cycle6.outputStyle.test.ts assert the warning shape directly.' Optionally tighten the assertion to also expect result.warning?.kind==='output-style-plugin-not-enabled' so the cycle-1 baseline matches the cycle-6 invariant.
evidence: // Silent disable path — must not throw, warning is optional but typically absent.

title: selectPath corrupt+archivedTo recovery silently drops user errors[]
description: When loadState returns kind:'corrupt' with archivedTo defined, selectPath falls through to mint a fresh state and returns {ok:true, ...} per AC-7.2. This is correct. However, the user receives NO indication that their previous state was corrupted and archived — selectPath's SelectPathResult shape has no warnings field (only ok / personalizationPrompts / errors). The contract at AC-7.2 requires the slot be treated as 'absent' but does not forbid surfacing a recovery notice. From the user's perspective they invoked selectPath and got back a clean ok:true result; they have no way to learn that their state.json was archived to .sui-deepbook-course/state.corrupt-*.json.
impact: Surprise factor for the user: their cursor history silently disappears with no diagnostic. start.ts already emits a 'state-corrupt' warning with archivedTo on the same path (start.ts L54-67), so the channel exists — selectPath just doesn't use it. T-309 asserts the recovery succeeds but does NOT assert any warning is surfaced, so this gap is not caught by tests. Low severity because the contract permits it (AC-7.2 only mandates fresh-state minting), but it's a UX seam.
recommendation: Either (a) extend SelectPathResult with an optional warnings: StateWarning[] field and push a state-corrupt warning when stateResult.archivedTo is defined, or (b) document explicitly in selectPath.ts that the recovery path is silent because start.ts already emitted the warning on the prior call. Option (a) is more robust because it does not depend on call-ordering assumptions.

title: T-310 archive de-dup test runs only 3 starts; concurrency case unverified
description: T-310 asserts archive de-duplication for 3 SEQUENTIAL runStart calls on identical corrupt bytes. The contract AC-7.2 mandates 'after N consecutive /start calls on the same corrupt input MUST equal 1'. The dedup mechanism is content-hash-based (state.ts L114-144 findExistingArchive), reading the state directory and computing SHA-256 over each existing archive's bytes. There is no file-locking around findExistingArchive → writeFile, so two concurrent loadState calls on identical corrupt bytes could both observe an empty directory and both write archives — defeating the dedup invariant. The test exercises only sequential calls, so this race is not caught.
impact: A user running `/start` twice in rapid succession (e.g. a script that retries) could still produce 2 archives. In practice MCP tool invocations from a single Claude session are serialized, so the race is unlikely — but the contract's wording 'after N consecutive /start calls' does not exclude concurrent calls. Low severity because (a) the practical attack surface is narrow and (b) the wx flag on writeFile (state.ts L171) would cause the second writer to fail with EEXIST, surfacing the contention rather than silently double-archiving.
recommendation: Add a test that fires loadState concurrently against the same corrupt seed (Promise.all of two loadState calls) and asserts the directory still has exactly one state.corrupt-*.json. If the wx-flag race is the chosen mitigation, document it explicitly in state.ts archiveCorruptFile and assert archiveCorruptFile rejects gracefully on EEXIST (currently the rejection bubbles out as 'corrupt without archivedTo' in classifyCorrupt L100-106, which is acceptable but undocumented).

title: scenarios.json AC mapping for E-008 unchanged — covers_contract still says AC-2.3
description: Cycle 6 AC-5 (H005) adds the 'Sui CLI in 1.63.2–1.64.1' precondition to E-008 and pins the contract anchor as AC-2.5 (per the cycle-6 contract.md L51, T-313's ac_anchor). However, E-008's covers_contract array in scenarios.json still reads ['AC-2.3'] — unchanged from cycle 5. The cycle-6 contract says AC-2.5 governs the precondition addition. The covers_contract field is now stale relative to the new precondition.
impact: E2E coverage indexing in any tool that reads covers_contract (e.g. for a coverage matrix between scenarios and acceptance criteria) will not see E-008 covering AC-2.5, only the original AC-2.3. This is mostly cosmetic for now because cycle-e2e-pass.sh and the cycle-6 tests don't read covers_contract. Low severity, but it's a documentation seam between two adjacent ACs that share a scenario.
recommendation: Update E-008's covers_contract to ['AC-2.3', 'AC-2.5'] so the precondition→AC trace is explicit. Alternatively, document in the file header that the precondition list is the authoritative AC-2.5 carrier and covers_contract reflects the deploy-flow ACs only.

title: cycle-e2e-pass.sh always returns exit 0 — non-skip failures not surfaced
description: The script terminates with `exit 0` unconditionally at L125. The `case` dispatch at L116-123 calls run_e008 (which returns 0 on skip and 0 on a successful manual-check passthrough) for SCENARIO_FILTER='E-008' or '' (empty), and prints an INFO line for any other filter — but never returns non-zero. The contract at AC-5 says 'on a host with sui in range, E-008 still runs as before' implying the success/failure signal of the real run must propagate. The shebang has `set -euo pipefail` so a deploy-all command that exits non-zero would cause the script to abort, but since run_e008 never spawns the real deploy (L107: 'Skipping actual pnpm deploy-all here — manual run required in full e2e environment'), the script can never produce a non-zero exit even on a host with valid Sui+Docker.
impact: A CI integrator who runs cycle-e2e-pass.sh expecting exit-status semantics ('0=pass/skip, 1=fail') will get a false-PASS signal for any genuine E-008 failure. T-314 asserts the skip exit is 0, but no test asserts a real-fail exit is non-zero. The contract wording 'On a host with sui in range, E-008 still runs as before' is partially false: the script delegates to a manual run rather than spawning the real deploy. This is a documented hand-wave (the L106-108 INFO lines say so), but it means the script is not yet a complete E-008 implementation.
recommendation: Either (a) implement the real pnpm deploy-all spawn under a guarded code path and propagate its exit code, or (b) tighten the contract wording / scripts header to say the script is a precondition-gate-only stub and the actual deploy is performed by a separate harness. The current state — 'pretends to run E-008 but always exits 0' — is the worst of both worlds for CI consumers.
evidence:
echo "INFO: Skipping actual pnpm deploy-all here (manual run required in full e2e environment)."
  echo "PASSED: scenario=E-008 (preconditions met; run manually in full e2e environment)"
  return 0

title: Anti-weakening verification: T-014 amendment is STRONGER than original (good)
description: Anti-weakening check on T-014 amendment. Original (cycle 1): asserted bare {ok:false, warning:<falsy>} on the plugin-disabled branch. New (cycle 6): asserts ok===false AND warning is defined AND warning.kind==='output-style-plugin-not-enabled' AND warning.message contains both the plugin key and 'claude plugins enable'. The new assertion is STRICTLY STRONGER — the original would pass with the new code (because warning is no longer falsy, the original would have failed; that's why the amendment was needed). Conclusion: amendment is justified by AC-1.1 / H002 and asserts MORE than the original. Anti-weakening intent preserved.
impact: None — this is a healthy amendment.
recommendation: No action required. Filing as info to confirm the orchestrator's anti-weakening claim in synthesis-notes is verified.

title: Anti-weakening verification: T-275 amendment INVERTS contract — justified by AC-7.2
description: Anti-weakening check on T-275 amendment. Original (cycle 1): asserted selectPath short-circuits with {ok:false} when loadState returns kind:'corrupt'. New (cycle 6): asserts {ok:true} AND saveState.toHaveBeenCalled(). This is a direct INVERSION of the assertion — but it is justified by AC-7.2 / H003: cycle 6 explicitly mandates that corrupt+archivedTo MUST mint a fresh state instead of refusing. The mock setup in the new T-275 includes archivedTo:'/x' which triggers the new branch in selectPath.ts L88. The original assertion CANNOT hold under the new contract (it would directly contradict AC-7.2). The amendment is necessary and the new test matches the inverted behavior precisely. Anti-weakening intent preserved (inversion is contract-mandated, not weakening).
impact: None — this is the canonical case for orchestrator amendments where a later cycle inverts an earlier behavior. The synthesis-notes correctly flags this as a baseline-rebase, not a softening.

title: Anti-weakening verification: T-281 amendment is STRONGER than original
description: Anti-weakening check on T-281 amendment. Original (cycle 4): asserted phases 2 & 3 prompts contain literal 'TBD' / 'cycle 5' (placeholder shape). New (cycle 6): asserts phases.length===3, phases[1].id==='p2-retry', phases[2].id==='p3-poll', specific target_ranges, verification.mode values, and that prompt contains '{{ poll_interval_ms }}'. The new assertion is STRICTLY STRONGER — it pins down the phase ids, target ranges, verification modes, AND the substitution placeholder, where the original only asserted a TBD-shaped prompt. The schema validity check (validatePhases) is preserved. Anti-weakening intent preserved (amendment is justified by AC-6.3 / H001 and asserts MORE than the original).

title: Anti-weakening verification: T-050 amendment preserves intent under new substrate
description: Anti-weakening check on T-050 amendment. Original (cycle 1): asserted 'two corruptions in same wall-clock second produce two distinct archive paths' using IDENTICAL bytes for both calls. New (cycle 6): same name and intent ('distinct archive paths under same-second timing') but uses two DIFFERENT byte sequences. The change is necessary because cycle 6 H003 introduces content-hash dedup — IDENTICAL bytes now collapse to ONE archive (asserted by T-310). The original test's IDENTICAL-bytes setup would now produce ONE archive, contradicting both the new dedup invariant AND the original's distinct-paths claim. The amendment splits the invariant into two: T-050 (distinct bytes → distinct archives, timestamp-disambiguation branch) and T-310 (identical bytes → one archive, dedup branch). Both invariants together are STRICTLY STRONGER than the original single invariant. Anti-weakening intent preserved.
impact: None — the amendment correctly rebases the invariant onto the new content-hash substrate without losing the original A18 timestamp-disambiguation property.
recommendation: No action required. The pair (T-050, T-310) collectively asserts more than the original T-050 alone.

title: AC-3 bytes-equality invariant verified end-to-end
description: AC-3 / AC-7.2 mandates 'archive bytes MUST match the corrupt state.json's pre-recovery bytes exactly.' Verified: archiveCorruptFile (state.ts L146-173) writes `content` (the raw string read from state.json at L29) directly via fsPromises.writeFile; no re-encoding, no JSON re-stringify, no pretty-printing. T-311 captures original bytes via fs.readFileSync(stateFile) (Buffer, no encoding) BEFORE invoking loadState, then compares the archive buffer via Buffer.compare === 0. Test passes per green.log. Including a non-ASCII tail byte (\xff) in T-311's seed proves the path preserves bytes through Node's UTF-8 read → write cycle.
recommendation: No action required. Filing as info because this was the cluster-C009 invariant from Phase F and is now demonstrably preserved.

title: AC-1 / AC-6 verified: phases.json renames + content packs + cursor advance all hold
description: AC-1 (H001) and AC-6 (E-004 ladder traversal) end-to-end check: (a) phases.json declares p2-retry with target_range 103-114 (L31-39) and p3-poll with target_range 116-145 (L58-66) — confirmed. (b) p3-spot-1 prompt contains literal '{{ poll_interval_ms }}' (L67) — confirmed. (c) substitutePromptOnly (personalization.ts L129-139) substitutes via /\{\{\s*([a-zA-Z_][a-zA-Z0-9_]*)\s*\}\}/g which matches both '{{ poll_interval_ms }}' (with spaces) and '{{poll_interval_ms}}' (without). T-303 asserts both forms are gone post-substitution and '5000' is present. (d) All 8 new content files present and non-empty (T-305 green). (e) p3 reference.md preserves '{{ poll_interval_ms }}' so requestHint-time substitution can fire (verified at reference.md L28). (f) T-315 confirms ladder rung-3 advance from p1-spot-1 → p2-retry/p2-spot-1 works with the renamed phase ids.
impact: None — all AC-1 and AC-6 properties hold.

title: AC-4 probeOpts threading verified — both runProbe and runDeployRemediation forwarders use it
description: AC-4 (H004) verified: runPreflightProbe.ts L36 calls runProbe(probeId, probeOpts[probeId] ?? {}) for the initial probe, and L56-58 passes a forwarder closure (pid) => runProbe(pid, probeOpts[pid] ?? {}) into runDeployRemediation. Both call sites use the same lookup pattern, so the harness's withDockerStub registration on probeOpts['docker-running'] flows through both the initial probe AND the precondition gate during remediation. T-312 (green) asserts the warning's probeId is exactly 'docker-running' (not 'sui-cli-version' or a host probe), confirming the gate stops at the first failing precondition with the harness-injected stub. The test also asserts no spawnSpy call matches cmd='pnpm' + args including 'deploy-all', confirming the gate short-circuits before runRealDeploy.
